Strong scaling and weak scaling are two fundamental concepts in high-performance computing (HPC). In strong scaling, a system is designed to handle a large number of parallel processes simultaneously. In weak scaling, a system is designed to handle a large number of parallel processes simultaneously while also scaling the number of processors used to handle each process.

Strong scaling is commonly used in HPC applications where the number of parallel processes is limited by the number of processors available. For example, in a supercomputer, a single processor can handle a limited number of tasks, while a cluster of processors can handle many tasks simultaneously. In this case, strong scaling is used to maximize the number of parallel processes that can be handled by the system.

On the other hand, weak scaling is commonly used in HPC applications where the number of parallel processes is not limited by the number of processors available. For example, in a supercomputer, a single processor can handle a limited number of tasks, while a cluster of
